title: Five Things You Can Feel, Four You Can Hear
subtitle: A grounding meditation for landing in the present moment before sleep
excerpt: A five-minute sensory grounding meditation for Day 8 of the sleep arc. The practice moves attention through the senses in sequence, counting what can be felt, heard, and smelled right now. The counting gives the mind something concrete to do while it returns to the present.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. Built from the Day 8 theme of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025, ISBN 9798269649795): grounding in the present moment. The 5-4-3-2-1 sensory grounding technique is widely used in anxiety and grounding practice; no single originator.

practice.whenToUse: When the mind keeps drifting away from the present moment at bedtime — use this lying down in bed.

content.text: A short sensory grounding meditation for Day 8. Use it lying down in bed. The practice pulls attention back to the body by working through the senses one at a time — giving the mind something concrete to do instead of running.
Lie down. Eyes closed. One slow breath in, then out. You're going to move through your senses — slowly, deliberately, naming what's actually there.

Five things you can feel. Not emotions — physical sensations. The weight of the duvet. The temperature of the pillow. Your heels against the mattress. The fabric of your pyjamas on your arms. The warmth of your own body heat. Name each one inwardly, slowly.

Four things you can hear. The quiet of the room. Traffic in the distance. The sound of your own breathing. Someone in another room, or the house settling. Name each one as you find it. You're not looking for perfect silence — you're finding out what's actually there.
Three things you can smell. This one is harder. The pillow. The room itself. Your own skin. If nothing comes, that's fine — stay with the searching for a moment. The searching is the practice.
Two more things you can feel. Go back to the body. The weight of your hands. The rise and fall of your chest. Something small and specific.
One thing you can feel right now that is comfortable. Find it. Stay with it.
Let the attention rest on the body without directing it anywhere. No next task. The mind is in the room now.
